q: The simplest organic compounds that contain only the elements carbon and hydrogen are called hydrocarbons.
The characteristic odour of cherries is due to methoxymethane.
Ethers have the general formula $C_{n}H_{2n+2}O$.
In a symmetrical ether, the two alkyl groups are different ($R$ and $R'$).
Solubility of ethers decreases as the number of carbon atoms increases.
There is strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding between ether molecules.
Aldehydes and ketones are organic compounds which contain the carbonyl group, $>C=O$.
In ketones, a carbonyl group is located at the end of a carbon chain.
The location of the substituent in the carbon chain of aldehydes is indicated by the Greek letters $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$.
Primary alcohols give ketones when heated with an oxidising agent.
Secondary alcohols give ketones when heated with an oxidising agent.
The ketone product must be distilled off immediately to prevent further oxidation.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids with common oxidising agents such as potassium permanganate.
Ketones are easily oxidised under mild conditions like aldehydes.
Formalin (40 % aqueous solution of methanal) is used as a disinfectant and a preservative for biological specimens.
Propylamine is a secondary amine according to IUPAC nomenclature.
Carboxylic acids form a homologous series with the general formula $C_{n}H_{2n}O_{2}$.
The IUPAC name of acetic acid is methanoic acid.
Ethanoic acid is formed by the oxidation of ethanol.
Solubility of carboxylic acids increases with the larger hydrocarbon portion.
Carboxylic acids react with carbonates to give salt, water and carbon dioxide.
Esters are carboxylic acid derivatives in which the carbonyl group has been replaced by an alkoxy group.
The process of forming an ester from a carboxylic acid and alcohol is known as esterification.
Acidic hydrolysis of an ester is a non-reversible reaction.
Amines are classified as primary ($1^{\circ}$), secondary ($2^{\circ}$), and tertiary ($3^{\circ}$), depending on how many alkyl groups are attached to the nitrogen.
Primary and secondary amines have lower boiling points than those of alkanes of a similar molecular mass.
Amides are carboxylic acid derivatives in which the $-OH$ of the carboxylic acid has been replaced by $-NH_{2}, -NHR,$ or $-NR_{2}$.
All simple amides are liquids under standard conditions.
The addition of ammonia ($NH_{3}$) to a carboxylic acid forms an amide through a condensation reaction.
Ethers are used as surgical anaesthetic agents because they are nonflammable.
The characteristic odour of cherries is due to benzaldehyde.
Alcohols can be used as solvents in marker pens but not in medicines.
Acetone is the main component of nail polish remover.
Diethyl ether has the IUPAC name methoxyethane.
Ethoxyethane and water are formed when ethanol is treated with a limited amount of sulphuric acid and heated to $140 ^{\circ}C$.
Methoxymethane and methoxyethane are colourless liquids at room temperature.
Aldehydes and ketones form a homologous series and have the general formula $C_{n}H_{2n}O$.
The suffix -ol is used to designate the characteristic stem of an aldehyde in the IUPAC system.
Secondary alcohols must be heated with an oxidising agent to produce a ketone.
Addition of water to ethyne in the presence of $H_{2}SO_{4}$ and $HgSO_{4}$ produces propanone.
An alkane lacks functional groups whereas an alkene and an alkyne have functional groups.
Infrared (IR) spectroscopy is a destructive technique used to identify compounds.
Alkenes react with $1\% KMnO_{4}$ solution resulting in decolourisation.
Alkanes decolourise $1\% KMnO_{4}$ solution.
Passing an alkyne through ammoniacal $Cu_{2}Cl_{2}$ solution produces a red coloured precipitate.
Fehling's solution is used to distinguish between different types of ethers.
When blue Fehling’s solution is warmed with an aldehyde, a reddish-brown precipitate is formed.
Ketones give a reddish-brown precipitate with Fehling’s solution.
The 2,4-DNP test produces a deep-orange precipitate in the presence of a carbonyl group.
IR spectroscopy is the study of the absorption of ultraviolet light by a compound.
Frequency (\nu) is the number of wave crests that pass by a given point in one second.
High frequencies are associated with long wavelengths.
Homonuclear diatomic molecules ($N_{2}$) which have no dipole moments are IR inactive.
Heteronuclear diatomic molecules ($CO$) are IR inactive.
Stretching is a vibration that occurs along the bond line and changes the bond length.
Bending is a vibration that occurs between two bonds and keeps the bond angle constant.
Wavenumber is the reciprocal of the wavelength.
Light atoms vibrate at lower frequencies than heavier atoms.
Multiple bonds vibrate at higher frequencies than single bonds.
The O-H bond of alcohols usually has an absorption in the range of $2500-3300 cm^{-1}$.
The O-H bond of carboxylic acids occurs in the range of $2500-3300 cm^{-1}$.
C=O stretching vibration for amides and ketones occurs in the range of $1050-1410 cm^{-1}$.
N-H stretch is narrow and weak while the O-H stretch is broad and strong.
Polarity of the bond and number of bonds do not influence the intensity of an absorption band.
Wavelength is the distance from any point on one wave to the corresponding point on the next wave.
Wavenumber is the number of wave crests that pass in one second.
Spectroscopy is the study of the interaction of matter and electromagnetic radiation.
Heteronuclear diatomic molecules possess zero dipole moments and thus give rise to IR absorption.
An IR spectrum is a plot of the percent transmittance or absorbance of radiation versus the wavenumber of the radiation transmitted.
It takes less energy to stretch a bond than to bend it.
A macromolecule is a very large molecule that contains thousands of atoms or more.
Carbohydrates and nucleic acids are synthetic polymers manufactured in industry.
A homopolymer is a polymer that consists of only one kind of monomers.
A copolymer is a polymer formed from only one kind of monomers.
Addition polymerisation occurs when unsaturated monomers join together without losing any molecules or atoms.
Condensation polymerisation occurs with the addition of a small molecule such as water.
The simplest addition polymer is polyethene or polyethylene (PE).
The number of ethene molecules in each polyethene molecule varies from 100 to 300.
Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is produced by the addition polymerisation of chloroethene.
In polyalkenes made of one type of monomer, the repeat unit is different from the monomer unit.
Addition polymers lose plasticity and elasticity and become brittle at low temperatures.
Polythene is a natural polymer found in silk.
Terylene is an example of condensation polymers formed from terephthalic acid and ethylene glycol.
Terylene is a polyamide because it contains many amide linkages.
Nylon 6,6 is formed from the reaction of adipic acid and 1,6-diaminohexane.
Nylon is a polyester because it contains many ester linkages.
Proteins are made from thousands of amino acid monomers.
There are 50 different amino acids commonly found in proteins.
The covalent bond formed between two amino acids is called a peptide bond (amide link).
Condensation polymers often have higher molecular masses than addition polymers.
Plastics are non-biodegradable and can take hundreds of years to decompose.
Burning PVC produces non-toxic gases like oxygen.
Sea animals such as turtles often mistake plastic bags for food and choke on them.
Soil pollution by plastics only lasts for a few months.
The easiest way to deal with plastics is to reuse or recycle them.
Recycling symbols on plastics consist of three arrows around one of ten numbers.
Biodegradable plastics contain small amounts of starch granules to help decompose naturally.
Polylactic acid (PLA) is a petroleum-based plastic.
Photodegradable plastics incorporate carbonyl groups ($C=O$) along their chain to absorb energy from sunlight.
Starch is a synthetic polymer used in the production of PVC pipes.
Frying pans are coated with a layer of teflon because it is heat-resistant and has non-stick properties.
Polystyrene (styrofoam) is a heavy and durable plastic used for flooring mats.
PET stands for polyethylene terephthalate.
V/PVC stands for polyvinyl acetate.
LDPE stands for low-density polyethylene.
PS stands for polypropylene.
Adipic acid and 1,6-diaminohexane are the monomers of nylon 6,6.
The repeat unit of polyethene is $-(CH_{2}-CHCl)-$.
The presence of polar functional groups on condensation polymer chains often enhances chain-chain attractions.
Terylene is very weak and is not used in textiles.
